# Breaking Changes from Beta 6

### New effect names

These effects were renamed:

`faint` is now `dim`
`conceal` is now `hidden`
`reverse` is now `inverse`
`blink_fast` is now `blink`
`underline` is now `underl`

### Drop blink_fast

Blink fast doesn't seem to be supported by most modern terminals. It was dropped for a cleaner api and possible unwanted side-effects.

### Merge resetters for bold and dim

It turns out that most terminal use the same reset sequence for both `bold` and `dim`. That means `rs.bold` and `rs.dim` have to be merged into `rs.bold_dim` and `rs.dim_bold`. This sucks, but since that's how terminals do it, I don't see a better way to solve this in `sty`.

That measn `rs.bold`, `rs.b`, `rs.dim` (formaly `rs.faint`) were dropped in favor of `rs.bold_dim` and `rs.dim_bold`.

I'm sorry, but this makes no sense whatsoever.  If I write code like this:

    async def spam():
            async with defer_cancellation:
                     await coro1()
                     await coro2()
                     await coro3()

My expectation is that this code is guaranteed to not receive a cancellation in that block of statements.  No matter what `coro1()`, `coro2()`, or `coro3()` are doing internally. End of story.

As Curio is currently implemented, ANY use of `allow_cancellation` inside `coro1()`, `coro2()` or `coro3()` violates this guarantee.  The reason is that a cancellation request is supposed to kill the entire task regardless of the call stack.  A cancellation request also can't be ignored.  So, even if one of those coroutines "caught" the cancellation request, it is not allowed to just eat it and keep on  going.  That's not cancellation.

The bottom line is that any use of `allow_cancellation` has the potential to make `defer_cancellation` fail to work as advertised--making it rather worthless as a feature in my opinion.

The whole situation is further complicated by the fact that timeouts are intermingled with cancellation as a concept.   Unlike a true cancellation, a timeout can be caught and acted upon.  So, that's it's own problem on top of everything else.

So, with all of this, I'm not exactly sure how I'd want to proceed.  I just know that I don't want to be fielding a bunch of bug reports regarding tasks being mysteriously cancelled despite the fact that they're in the middle of a `defer_cancellation` block at the time.

Having spent a while trying to fix this, there are a series of challenges:
 - In 2.6, the things you get back from `gzip.open` / `bz2.BZ2File` are missing most of the methods that `TextIOWrapper` depends on, so it's not a simple as copying over `read1` 
 - In 2.7 the `gzip` version of the workaround above works, but `bz2.BZ2File` is a C-like thing (not a pythonic object), so you can't assign arbitrary attributes
 - In <3.3, `gzip`/`bz2` don't have an `open` method (and the `File` versions are still missing methods such as `read1` which are required to get `TextIOWrapper` to work), and if you don't call `open` in 3.3+ with `t` then newline behaviour isn't what you want

One option would be to use external packages that work around this issue:
 - [bz2file](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/bz2file) (claims to provide a modern API-compatible `BZ2File` and `open`)
 - [backports.lzma](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/backports.lzma) (a backport of the 3.3+ `lzma` module which therefore has a modern API, even on earlier pythons, but requires a C module, you'd probably only want to make use of it if it was already available)
 - ?? (haven't found an equivalent for `gzip`)

So is the problem that you can't match SRC's table-defined filter with
closed form?  If he included a pre-defined filter instead of
calculating it on the fly, that strongly suggests to me that it can't
be exactly generated from closed form, which suggests it was generated
through some kind of gradient-descent scheme that is too slow or bulky
(or manual) to include in the shipping code.   If you're trying to
reproduce his results without resorting to a stored table, I'd guess
that's futile.

It's expected that the result of an optimal gradient-descent scheme
would be very close to Kaiser (or other heuristic-but-near-optimal)
approach; that's why the heuristic approaches are acceptable.  But it
doesn't mean that the two windows were designed by similar approaches,
just that they both converged on (approximately) the same optimum.

But I'm sure you know this, so I'm not clear what you're looking for.
My gut is that distortion < 100 dB isn't possible with any kind of
material bug.  Also, I wouldn't expect any reasonable closed-form (but
heuristic, i.e. windowing) design approach to do better than this
without really huge overengineering.

It's not so straight forward to support runtime polymorphic networks given
the existing code base.  Obviously this makes importing caffe models
problematic but there are a bunch of other considerations.  For instance,
importing caffe models at some level foists the complexities and design
problems of caffe onto software that supports reading them.

For example, if you write a new layer type, how does that layer type get
automatically serialized when saving a network?  The template system in use
now makes that automatic.  But with runtime polymorphism you need some kind
of global registry/factory thing that exists somewhere in dlib and performs
mappings between magic strings and classes.  This means that users who want
to implement new layer types need to edit the dlib code.  A major design
goal of this API is to let users create new loss layers, computational
layers, and solvers without needing to understand or even look at the dlib
internals.  A lot of the API decisions are based on what makes the
interface a user needs to implement for new layer creation simple, and if
you look at it you will find that it's far simpler than the interface in
caffe, while also being very general.

Also, the entire structure of template alias statements and tag
introspection doesn't work with runtime polymorphism.

Since it takes days to train these models, needing to recompile to change
the network architecture isn't a big deal.  You recompile every few days.
The main downside is not being able to import models from other tools.  But
this is a short term problem which is easily remedied by putting more dlib
models on the internet.  Supporting import from other tools like caffe is a
never ending odesy of varying and incompatible formats and design
decisions.

What models from caffe do you want to use?

For the tests, the fix is to be explicit about the encoding and don't let Python to do the encoding/decoding for you.

So in this code, for example don't pass Unicode to the Python low level API as this will produce various encodings

```
os.mkdir(join(TEST_PATH, 'subdir', 'unidir\u018F'))
```

but instead be explicit and pass bytes which are already encoded

```
path = join(TEST_PATH, 'subdir', 'unidir\u018F')
os.mkdir(path.encode('utf-8')
```

---------

For general usage, I don't know if there is a fix.

scandir should not try to do any smart thing with the file names and just pass them as bytes (without trying to decode them)

Linux / Unix filesystems are just bytes... so you can store whatever you want as the file name and in whatever format you want

Things can get messy and in the same folder have an UTF-8 encoded name, ASCII and EBCDIC

This is not a FilterPy bug. All of the filters must have the same state dimension. If you think about it, you'll see why. The IMM is blending the states of all of the filters in the filter bank; if they were of different shapes or had different interpretations (x[2] is acceleration in one, but a coefficient of friction in another), then there is no way to blend the states together to get an estimate.

Thank you for reporting, I am personally grateful for this observation since it identifies a few issues that I need to fix in the new test suite for v3.0 (#199). The assertion checks that there is not more than a 5% variation between the targeted time and the output, in this case your test shows 6.7%. Over the span of 1.4 months, that represents a difference of 2.8 days. 

I believe that this is a bug in the test case rather than in parsedatetime. The test assertion uses the following logic to calculate months:
```
        if months:
            delta += datetime.timedelta(days=30 * months)
```
Whereas `Calendar.inc()`, which I believe is used in these deltas, calculates month deltas based on the calendar (1 month before March 10 is always Feb 10 regardless of leap years), then adds the partial months. 

The 5% rule for deltas is problematic, that can represent a huge variation from expectation when the range is months or years. Fortunately the new test suite uses different logic for deltas and as a result this test passes.

To help determine whether there is actually a bug in parsedatetime, here is a comparison between 2016 and 2017:
```
>>> cal.parse('1.4 months ago', sourceTime=datetime.datetime(2016,3,10,9,24,0))
(time.struct_time(tm_year=2016, tm_mon=1, tm_mday=29, tm_hour=19, tm_min=0, tm_sec=0, tm_wday=4, tm_yday=29, tm_isdst=-1), 1)
>>> cal.parse('1.4 months ago', sourceTime=datetime.datetime(2017,3,10,9,24,0))
(time.struct_time(tm_year=2017, tm_mon=1, tm_mday=30, tm_hour=4, tm_min=36, tm_sec=0, tm_wday=0, tm_yday=30, tm_isdst=-1), 1)
```

I'm going to close this only because I really don't think it is a pyodbc issue as opposed to a SQL Server driver issue.  If you want to examine this further, can you provide an ODBC trace file?

A quick test under Linux confirms that pyodbc simply constructs the connection string using *all* supplied keyword arguments and passes that to the driver manager:

```python
cnxn = pyodbc.connect(
    Bacon="yummy",
    Driver="FreeTDS",
    Server="192.168.1.128",
    Port="49242",
    Database="myDb",
    TDS_Version="7.3",
    UID="sa",PWD="whatever"
)
```

```
[ODBC][3738][1535029010.553899][SQLDriverConnectW.c][290]
        Entry:
            Connection = 0xef21d0
            Window Hdl = (nil)
            Str In = [Bacon=yummy;Driver=FreeTDS;Server=192.168.1.128;Port=49242;Database=myDb;TDS_Version=7.3;UID=sa;PWD=whatever;][length = 109]
```

It is up to the driver to ignore (or complain about) connection string arguments that it doesn't understand.

That makes sense, since pyodbc has to deal with many different ODBC drivers with many different connection string arguments, so it doesn't really know (or particularly care) if the keyword arguments are valid for the ODBC driver in question. It just passes along what it was given in the `.connect` call.

Which exact version of Python 3.5 are you using: 3.5.4, 3.5.3, etc.?
Similarly, are you using pyODBC 4.0­ **.0** or some other 4.0.x version (in which case, please specify x)? You can `print(pyodbc.version)` to determine this.

I totally agree that using an open source project in the core of a SaaS business without giving anything back to the community is a shame and I'd not be happy with it as well.

But there are other scenarios where GPL and variations could become a problem. Say you're using TPOT in a SaaS, meaning no packaging. All good. A few years later this big customer asks you to run your software on premise. Now you have a big problem: does GPL allow you to do it without having to compromise your intellectual property in other areas of the project that interacts with the GPL software? Or say you decide to patent other parts of your software that interact or rely on TPOT... There are lots of subtleties with GPL and the like that make it harder to answer these questions.

You might end up with a relieving "yes", but my point is: you can't be really sure without spending reasonable money with good lawyers to study your particular use-case. When you're starting up, you want to avoid this future liability AND avoid spending money with lawyers. Thus, the best for a small business is to stick with MIT, BSD or Apache 2.0. That's all I'm saying. LGPL will make it harder for SMEs to work with it. I'm not saying they can't work with it, I'm just saying: if there's an MIT/BSD alternative, I'll definitely stick to it.

Essentially, I think that this discussion has shown that the collection interface (`len` and indexing) is not a good match for soundfile, since not all soundfiles are seekable, and not all soundfiles have a definite length. Our inclusion of `__len__` stems from an earlier time when we supported indexing on soundfile objects.

Instead, we can use the file-like object interface. At the moment, we implement almost the full interface of [`io.IOBase`](https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/io.html#io.IOBase), except for `fileno`, `isatty`, `readable`, `readline`, `readlines`, `writable`, and `writelines`. We should be able to include at least `readable` and `writable`, and maybe `fileno` without much problems. To be more uniform, we would have to add a few errors when reading from a non-readable file or from a closed file etc.

In the `io.IOBase` interface, some methods return a value or throw an error if they are not applicable. If we implement `.frames` as a method, it would be normal behavior to throw an error if the file is not seekable.

Seeing that this would make the library more file-like and more consistent, I am in favor of removing `__len__`, and implementing some variant of `.frames`.

> If the client disconnects before the iterable is done, I see this message

> keeps iterating until is done, sucking CPU and RAM. Doing this several
> times I can bring down my server, just leaving broken connections behind me
> sucking resources.
>
> Expected result: If a client disconects, the iterable I provided WSGI to
> send data to the client must be disposed, not called anymore and,

> Potential clashes - if I do --ignore-names app.route, both @app.route decorated functions as well as app.route itself is ignored, right? Also, the other way around. This seems kind of unexpected, because you usually want one or the other, but not both.

We add an `@` symbol for adding distinguishing between ignoring decorators and the other stuff. e.g. for ignoring the functions decorated with `app.route`, the command would be:
```
vulture myproject.py --ignore-names "@app.route"
```
whereas, for ignoring a variable (say `app.route`), I'd just type in:
```
vulture myproject.py --ignore-names app.route
```

An aside, we could do a rough equivalent of the less than desirable `\X` implementation of `(?>\PM\pM*)`.  I imagine it would look something like: `(?:\PM\pM*(?!\pM))` which would prevent back tracking from stripping off trailing `\pM` to "fit" the pattern.  Now this implementation is naive, but was originally what Perl did:

```py
>>> bre.findall(r"(?:\PM\pM*(?!\pM))", "\r\r\n\u0301A\u0301")
['\r', '\r', '\ń', 'Á']
```

Compared to a proper implementation, you can see the weakness:

```py
>>> regex.findall(r"\X", "\r\r\n\u0301A\u0301")
['\r', '\r\n', '́', 'Á']
```

I have been experimenting with caching on the [cache-experimentation branch](https://github.com/takluyver/entrypoints/tree/cache-experimentation). I tested one implementation with a cache file stored in each `sys.path` directory (`make_cache.py` and `read_cache.py`) and one with a single cache file with directories as keys (`*_single_cache.py`).

**Cold start performance** - where the files it reads are not cached in memory by the OS - tested using a command from [here](https://wiki.openoffice.org/wiki/Cold-start-simulator), on a system with a spinning hard drive (not an SSD). This is a worst case for this kind of operation, but it's realistic.

- Loading entrypoints with no cache files: 4.5 seconds
- Loading with a cache per directory: 0.44 seconds
- Loading with a single cache: 0.40 seconds
- Running Python with a no-op file: 0.05 seconds

However, my system has a short `sys.path`, with only 7 directories on it. If you have more a longer sys.path, e.g. because you're using 'egg' installations, the difference between per-directory caches and a single cache will be bigger.

**Warm start performance** - with the files in memory - didn't change much, from about 110ms with no cache to about 85ms with a single cache.

-----

The CVE values are stored in the **opts.vulns** property on the banner which is where the website pulls the information from (the website is powered by the API). And to get a list of currently-tested vulnerabilities and their CVE values you can use the **vuln** facet. Here's a command-line solution for getting the list:

`shodan stats --facets vuln net:0/0`

Note that vulnerabilities starting with a "!" mean that the given service is NOT vulnerable to the CVE. I.e. a value of "!cve-2014-0160" means the service is NOT vulnerable to Heartbleed. We only add the "!" for a few vulnerabilities that we added a long time ago and aren't doing this anymore for the new ones being added. But we're keeping the "!" values around since a few customers depend on it and we want to maintain backwards-compatibility.

Ah. So what about a case like `{{cite web|url=''http://example.com''}}`—the "parent node" of the URL is the italic tag, but I assume you would want to get the `{{cite web}}` template. So maybe you are looking for the parent _template_ of a node?

But how about `{{quote|text=blah blah http://example.com blah blah blah}}`? I assume here you wouldn't want to get the `{{quote}}` template because it's not in a designated list of templates that you should be adding `{{dead link}}` after. Is it more specific to say you are looking for the parent template of a node _in a certain list of template names_?

There are more bizarre cases, like `{{cite web|url={{quote|text=http://example.com}}}}`. I imagine here you would want to find `{{cite web}}` rather than nothing. Again you can see how the logic gets convoluted. You might consider constructs like this one impossible, but you would be surprised what bizarre wikicode people decide to write. Still, it might be useful to ignore cases like this one for your sanity...

So there's a big difference between finding the arbitrary deepest node that contains a particular node and finding the deepest template (in a set of valid template names) that contains a particular node. I think this is what you are trying to do. Yes, you can use the first one to solve the second one, but solving the second straight away might be easier for you.

@jschneier `gs.py` is using Google Cloud Storage's [XML Interoperable API](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/interoperability) which uses keyed-hash message authentication code (a.k.a. developer keys) that is linked to your Google account. Interoperable API is really meant for migration to Google Cloud Storage. The biggest problem with the developer keys is security and privacy. Developer keys should not be shared with anyone as they can be used to gain access to other Google Cloud Storage buckets linked to your Google account.

I believe that this pull request depends on [`gcloud.storage.client.Client`](https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/0.18.1/storage-client.html) which would require using [OAuth 2.0](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/authentication) for Google Cloud Storage API authN and authZ.

This is my script:

    #!/usr/bin/env python
    from __future__ import print_function, absolute_import

    import fnmatch
    import os
    import sys

    from livereload import Server
    from livereload.watcher import INotifyWatcher
    from livereload.handlers import LiveReloadHandler


    def recursive_glob(rootdir='.', pattern='*'):
        return [os.path.join(walkdir, filename)
                for walkdir, _, filenames in os.walk(rootdir)
                for filename in filenames
                if fnmatch.fnmatch(filename, pattern)]

    try:
        watcher = INotifyWatcher()
    except Exception:
        watcher = None

    if len(sys.argv) > 1 and sys.argv[1] == 'livereload_only':
        # livereload only
        port = 35729
        application = None
        debug = False
    else:
        port = 8000
        from config.wsgi import application
        debug = True

    server = Server(application, watcher)

    for filepath in recursive_glob("socialee/static", "*.scss"):
        assert os.path.exists(filepath)
        print("Watching: %s" % filepath)
        # server.watch(filepath, 'make scss')
        # delay=forever: do not trigger livereload; done via css watches.
        # Ref: https://github.com/lepture/python-livereload/issues/63#issuecomment-60712396  # noqa
        server.watch(filepath, 'make scss', delay='forever')

    # Watch .css:
    for filepath in recursive_glob("socialee/static", "*.css"):
        assert os.path.exists(filepath)
        print("Watching: %s" % filepath)
        server.watch(filepath)

    for filepath in recursive_glob("socialee/templates", "*.html"):
        assert os.path.exists(filepath)
        print("Watching: %s" % filepath)
        server.watch(filepath)


    while True:
        try:
            # debug=False to prevent tornado from epolling for changed files to the
            # livereload app (does not use inotify).
            server.serve(port=port, debug=debug)
        except SyntaxError as e:
            print('ERROR_NOT_HAPPENING?: %s. Restarting.' % str(e))
            continue
        break

If you want to control permissions of `comments.Comment`, you should bind a permission logic to a comment model not to an article model even if you want to control a behavior for adding comment on the article model.

For example, if you want to allow guest users to add comments on `blogs.Article` but other models, the following two permission logics can handle the situation.

```python
# /comments/perms.py
class CommentPermissionLogic(PermissionLogic):
    def has_perm(self, user_obj, perm, obj=None):
        if perm not in ('comments.add_comment',):
            # Unknown permission is specified, just ignore
            return False
        # Login users can add a comment
        return user_obj.is_authenticated()

PERMISSION_LOGICS = (
    ('comments.Comment', CommentPermissionLogic()),
)

# /blogs/perms.py
# It is quite blogs.Article specified permission logic thus I would put the code under 'blogs'
class ArticleCommentPermissionLogic(PermissionLogic):
    def has_perm(self, user_obj, perm, obj=None):
        if perm not in ('comments.add_comment',):
            # Unknown permission is specified, just ignore
            return False
        if perm == 'comments.add_comment' and obj is not None:
            # Note:
            #   This is a special case. Usually 'add' permission does not have 'obj' while
            #   'add' permission is non object permission.
            #   To figure out which object is going to be commented, I defined this special case
            #
            #       If a object is specified to 'comments.add_comment' permission, treat that
            #       object as an object which going to be commented.
            #
            #   To establish this, you have to specify an object going to be commented below.
            #   Note that <obj> in the following represent an object going to be commented.
            #
            #       {% If user has 'comments.add_comment' of obj %}
            #
            if isinstance(obj, Article)
                # Everybody can add a comment to blogs.Article
                return True
        return False
PERMISSION_LOGICS = (
    ('comments.Comment', ArticleCommentPermissionLogic()),
)
```

I split the logic into two permission logic but If you want, you can join them together. I just prefer to make systems independent as much as possible (If you split the logic into two logics, at least `comments.Comment` is independent).

So out-of-date type stubs will not prevent people from using your module. Dear colleagues,

I think that the restriction that all threads must be greenthreads is too restrictive. Consider you have a working thread which performs mathematical operation using numpy. This operation may take a while and there is no possibility to call sleep function from there, but since numpy is implemented in C it releases GIL during calculations, so Python interpreter performs quite well in parallel with such calculations. So the nice way to do such calculations is to fork a thread with numpy code.

When math calculations finish I need to somehow notify the eventloop where socket io server lives in, so it can notify the user by sending a message. The problem is that I cannot find the proper way to invoke the code in the event loop from my working thread. I do realize that there is a workaround with listener based on using sleep function @gordol posted above. I think it is not the solution of the problem. It is polling which obviously contradicts to the whole idea of asynchronous approach.

The proper solution would be to provide the thread-safe method which accepts function to call from the event loop next time it is possible. Please, take a look how it is implemented in the Tornado server: http://www.tornadoweb.org/en/stable/ioloop.html#tornado.ioloop.IOLoop.add_callback . I think it is the solution of the problem discussed in this post.

Best regards,

```zsh
gcf () {

	done
	git commit $opts --fixup "${@:-HEAD}"
}
```

810 is not the international country code for Kazakhstan, it's the international dial prefix for Kazakhstan (i.e. what you would start dialling with to get to an international number when in Kazakhstan).

Kazakhstan is reached via international country code +7 (bundled in with the Russian Federation).

+810 would actually fall under +81, which is the country code for Japan.

I seem to have found a solution, though I don't know why it works.

If I change:

```
class AlbumViewSet(viewsets.ModelViewSet):
    queryset = Album.objects.all().order_by('year', 'rank').distinct('year')

    serializer_class = AlbumSerializer
```

to:

```
class AlbumViewSet(viewsets.ModelViewSet):
    album = Album.objects.all().order_by('year').distinct('year')
    queryset = Album.objects.filter(id__in=album)

    serializer_class = AlbumSerializer
```

... all works perfectly! Sorting, filtering, etc all work now. An extra query is required, but it's worth it.

> So, if we follow up with another reserve, Spark will now try to assign the N reserve tasks on N-1 nodes. It will throw various TFManager already started exceptions, but it will eventually complete because of Spark's Python worker pools. 

I think most of it makes sense except the above. As the executors are configured with only one core, it should only be able to run just one python worker, otherwise your PS task doesn't be able to block the executor, because the executor can spawn another python worker.

This script only manipulates consumer offsets. Its behavior depends on the offset storage used:
* `--storage zookeeper`: the offsets will be removed.
* `--storage kafka` (default): the script will commit a 0 offset, since Kafka doesn't provide an API to delete a committed offset explicitly. The offset will actually be removed when it will expire, which depends on the broker configuration.

Other group related information, such as active members, cannot be controlled via this script.

@dlstadther 

1) In our day-to-day, we have source tables that we read from and depending on some data in those source table, we output the result in a new or an existing schema. That said, if source A requires schema B to be created, we would initially have a task that runs named `CreateSchema` that would create the schema. Then we've reflected upon that and we thought to ourselves that maybe `S3CopyToTable` should own that behavior. You literally can't create a table if the schema doesn't exist, so it made sense in our eyes to create the schema under that situation.

So in summary; depending on our source tables data, we have to create different schemas to reflect the type of data it contains so that we can differentiate between multiple sources of data dynamically.

Another idea that we've used that in the past, but no more, would be that each developer would have their own set of schemas and the output of the tasks would get saved in their schema. Since this can yield to multiple schema creations, we felt like the pipeline should take care of that step instead of having to us, manually create the required schemas for each developer.

2) I can concur that there is a lot of checks happening under that query but I think that it kinds of make sense. Mostly because the task itself may not work as expected if it doesn't those checks. I think it's a necessary evil. Also, I don't think that those checks necessary deteriorate the performance by a sufficient amount that we have to re-think it, but that call is over this contribution. Maybe we could think about adding some flags to some checks so that it doesn't do them automatically for the people who want better performance?

On another subject, we've been using Luigi for more than a year now and we've made a lot of tweaks that felt like improvements to us. I took it upon myself to merge back with the community, so you'll probably see a few more PRs coming from us! :)

In my view that is not on the table. `pipenv add package`, if it were a CLI interface, would need to perform the intuitive action of adding the package to your Pipfile _without_ installing it (i.e. in contrast to `pipenv install`).  And while yarn and bundler may behave a certain way, `pipenv` is operating within an ecosystem which has had packages governed by an `install` verb for decades -- I don't see a good design reason to try and change that now just because some pieces of other communities have axed the `install <package>` option.  I also don't fully understand why they have chosen to take that approach

And while `yarn` doesn't have an `install` command, `npm` does; where `bundler` lacks one, `RubyGems` has one.  I'll note here that `cargo` provides an install command, etc etc.  It's just not really a valid way of arguing the point of keeping or getting rid of a command.  As for the use case for adding something to the pipfile but not installing it, I can see the possibility but I am guessing this isn't that high a priority

Thanks @ax3l for the reproducer. I've uploaded it as [this gist](https://gist.github.com/jaraco/a7877d3bca06dac12cb840da187a6aba). I then made a couple of revisions. The first was to specify a base image, which I suspect was necessary because I'm testing on macOS. At that point, I was able to run the build and get the error you described.

But I noticed one thing - that you're not in fact installing Cython system-wide; you're installing it in the [user site](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0370/) (`pip install --user`). And the way user-local works is it's shared across different Python installs... which clarifies the answer to the question, how is `import cython` succeeding.

In the next revision, I installed Cython system-wide, and the issue goes away. Now that Cython is installed only to the system Python 2.7, it no longer appears in the miniconda install of Python 2.7, and all is well.

In rev 4, I took another tack and disabled that user-local environment before invoking the build. This also bypasses the issue. One could alternatively have set `PYTHONNOUSERSITE=1`.

I think this demonstrates that the issue originates from an incorrect usage of user-local environments and presents a couple of plausible workarounds.

I do sympathize with the issue and thinking about the presented workaround in Setuptools, I'm reluctant to go with that approach because of the complexity of behavior it adds on import. If on the other hand cython has or could add a call to check that it's viable in this environment, like `cython.assert_viable()`, that is something I would be willing to add, something like:

```python
try:
    # Attempt to use Cython for building extensions, if available
    from Cython.Distutils.build_ext import build_ext as _build_ext
    __import__('cython').assert_viable()
except (ImportError, AssertionError):
    _build_ext = _du_build_ext
```

While I understand your problem, and I agree that some clarification in the docs would be useful, I'd prefer different wording. In my opinion, the Ansible scripts that use `#!/usr/bin/env python` are *wrong*, unless the Ansible documentation makes it clear that you need the correct Python interpreter on your `PATH` (in which case, running via a non-activated virtualenv's interpreter is not following their documentation). Typically when running a Python subprocess, you should use `subprocess.call([sys.executable, ...])` to be sure you're using the same interpreter as your main executable, if that's what you want.

Having said all this, I'd be happy with a comment in the documentation saying that some 3rd party scripts assume that Python is in `PATH` or that `VIRTUAL_ENV` is set - if you want to use such scripts without activating your virtualenv, you will need to ensure that you manually set the appropriate environment variables in your code.

Well, my mind is suitably boggled. I unfortunately won't have time to do a detailed review until late January (Merry Christmas, everyone! ;) but:

a) the performance benefit is substantial enough that I agree with @soupault that this should definitely be included one way or another very soon, but
b) we need to be *really* careful about the deprecation path. These single pixel changes may seem inconsequential in the overall scheme of things, but various downstream packages may depend on these things for their testing. In particular, we need to check that this new method gives *correct* results other than some floating point approximation errors. See [these comments](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/2847#issuecomment-339532801) in #2847 for what kind of differences are acceptable or not. In those comments, I downplay the effect of single pixel changes, but I do that in favour of *not* changing things. When we do want to change the output, we must be much more careful.

@jni No worries -- it took me years of strenuously thinking about this exact question to come to this viewpoint. It's far from obvious or intuitive... (The good news is that once one fully absorbs how numpy striding works for images, you can do amazing/absurd things. Like make an (x, y, 3, 3)-shaped **view** on an (x, y)-shaped image, where the sub-array [i, j, :, :] of the view is the 3x3 neighborhood centered on pixel [i, j] in the original image...)

Anyhow, yes, my vote would be for (M, N, P...) indexing. I agree that "n0, n1" etc is too unwieldy.

Further, I totally agree about your example being a clear instance of something that ought to be fixed: the library assumes that coordinates are specified in (x, y) tuples, but that the array will be indexed [y, x]. In contrast, this is where `ndimage.map_coordinates` shines. So fixing the transforms and so forth to work more like `ndimage` would be a big win for sure.

My only objection to `r, c` notation is that it still contains the implicit assumption that the first axis is "supposed" to be rows, and the second axis is "supposed" to be columns. But once things are consistent, changing from `r, c` to `m, n` is (mostly) just a find-and-replace. I don't think there's too much code, other than image IO and display, that would have to be changed to go from `r, c` (with the implicit assumption about what the axes mean), to `m, n` (where there is no assumption and you need to ask the user if a bit of code needs to care about what the axes mean).

Fixed it by including local storage backend as well

```
class CachedS3BotoStorage(S3BotoStorage):
    """
    S3 storage backend that saves the files locally, too.

    It's needed for static files and django-compressor to get along :)
    """
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super(CachedS3BotoStorage, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self.local_storage = get_storage_class(
            "compressor.storage.CompressorFileStorage")()

    def save(self, name, content):
        """Save both on S3 and locally."""
        name = super(CachedS3BotoStorage, self).save(name, content)
        self.local_storage._save(name, content)
        return name

    def path(self, name):
        """
        Return absolute path as saved in local stoarge backend.

        Required by django-inlinecss.
        """
        return self.local_storage.path(name)
```

Yep, you found a bug that's just because of circular logic writing the code for permissions. At first I couldn't replicate it, and that's sure to be the reason it was overlooked in the first place. I can't fully explain why it happened sometimes and not other times, but I can explain those times it happens.

Overriding the .get() method had unintended consequences when, 4 steps down in the stack trace, `_lookup` called it with the identity tuple. That's a problem when `create` is called and sometime later, due to whatever reasons, `get` is called, because they both start out writing the `endpoint` value. The 2nd time goes poorly because at that point the necessary data to do so is no longer there.

有2种办法解决这个问题：
1. 事先使用`os.environ`给设置名为`"HOME"`的环境变量，如`os.environ["HOME"] = "./"`，这样就`os.getenv("HOME")`得到的就是你设置的环境变量了
2. `os.getenv()`可以设置默认值，在没有找到环境变量时就取默认值。
可以把zone.py中的`home = os.getenv("HOME")`改下，如设置默认值为当前路径`home = os.getenv("HOME", "./")`，这样就可以正常运行了

@arichelle do you have gurobi installed by any chance? A dependent package of memote will try to load gurobi (`gurobipy` specifically) which will lead to a segmentation fault if not the exact version of python is used that gurobi was compiled against (at least that was the problem for me). This is probably an issue of using $PYTHONPATH so that the wrong version of Python will still see gurobipy. Can you check what the output of `echo $PYTHONPATH` is on your terminal? A solution to this is to not use $PYTHONPATH to install Python packages. Also virtualenv should be used ideally. Of course we're targeting non-Python experts with the memote command line app so we have an interest in making the installation as smooth as possible. If it was indeed gurobipy causing you troubles then we check for this edge case @Midnighter because probably quite a lot of modellers will have gurobi installed. Also some info from your technician on the Linux side of problems would be appreciated.

I've been hacking on getting the lazy loaded connection and have realized

- We don't want lazy loading in tests (unless we are testing lazy loading)
- Every time a fresh `DEFAULT_ENVIRON` object is `_Monkey`-patched in a test, the property is lazy loaded
- If the modules import the `DEFAULT_ENVIRON` object, patching it on the `_implicit_environ` namespace doesn't change the object that has been imported
- Being super careful to only import the `_implicit_environ` module (instead of the object) is not future proof

I am debating a base class that explicitly sets the attribute to `None`:

```python
class DatastoreBaseTest(unittest2.TestCase):

    def setUp(self):
        from gcloud.datastore import _implicit_environ
        self._replaced_default = _implicit_environ.DEFAULT_ENVIRON
        _implicit_environ.DEFAULT_ENVIRON = _implicit_environ.Environment()
        _implicit_environ.DEFAULT_ENVIRON.dataset_id = None

    def tearDown(self):
        from gcloud.datastore import _implicit_environ
        _implicit_environ.DEFAULT_ENVIRON = self._replaced_default
```

but this is again not future proof since it requires tests to inherit from it.

The issue is that in that case the old LaunchConfig would be deleted from the stack each time, since it no longer exists (unless you plan on adding a new LaunchConfiguration() resource each time?  In which case using the ref doesn't give you much).  Honestly, what I'd suggest, is keeping versioned copies of each template.  Store them in S3, and launch your stacks directly from there.  Then you can name the templates whatever you like (including timestamps) and you know that any change is fully represented there.

```
from pants.util.contextutil import environment_as
```

We're solving this with better error signaling, and somewhat redesigned config loading API to be more explicit about the loading process.

When user explicitly asks for a specific config file, if we fail to load the config from that file, we're raising either `dwave.cloud.exceptions.ConfigFileReadError` or `dwave.cloud.exceptions.ConfigFileParseError`.

For context, this is actually a fairly deep issue since `txaio` is based around a "lowest common denominator" papering-over of the differences between Twisted and asyncio. Twisted has a single global reactor, and so does txaio.

In this particular case, I think passing `loop=` onwards to `make_batched_timer` is definitely "more correct", but also this needs a more comprehensive story, I think. Another option is to configure txaio to use your loop instead of passing a `loop=` kwarg to the factory -- that is: `txaio.config.loop = loop`?

(You're right that "txaio" has ONE event-loop, but it can be any eventloop; the default is `asyncio.get_event_loop()`...)

No, the code is fine as it is - there is no security issue here.

There is a misunderstanding of the purpose of client-to-server WebSocket masking here. It was introduced into the spec for this specific scenario: JavaScript running in the browser coming from an untrusted source, attacking an *intermediary* which does not understand WebSocket by sending deliberately formed byte sequences over the wire.

For JS running on NodeJS, this masking thing is snake oil: one can send arbitrary byte streams over TCP using that environment anyway. Of course the same with any language that allows direct and full TCP socket access.

So key to understanding the spec understanding the attacker: "authors of malicious applications". There is no server-to-client masking (because servers "are trusted"), there is client-to-server masking because we have browsers which implementors are trusted, but which run potentially malicious code from 3rd parties. You cannot stop some C coded client app sending you arbitrary bytes. Same for Python. Or put differently, Python does not expose a scripting sub-environment that would run code less trusted than the Python code in the first place (which can send arbitrary bytes).

Consequently, AutobahnPython even provides a switch to turn off client-to-server masking (for clients) and to turn off the checking for client-to-server masking (for servers), as this saves some CPU cycles.

In fact, we recommend for production:

* always use TLS (for security and non-interference from network intermedaries)
* use above switches and save some CPU cycles (when using implementations that support that)

I am doing something similar that may be relevant here.  On may app side, tsfresh feature names are considered immutable once seen.  I was glad to see that 0.3.0 only added and did not change.  That said in my application I have to map tsfresh feature names to an id due to database relationships.

Further to this in my app tests, I test tsfresh, to ensure that the tsfresh version is calculating the exact same values and feature names by calculating the features of a known static timeseries.  Each version then has a baseline in the tests too.  That way if you guys do not think feature names should be immutable or decide to change one, it will be caught on the tests and I can assess what to do :)

All that said, on my side I have something similar that I wanted for all possible pytz timezones.  So I automatically build a .rst page in my documentation build that have contains all the possible pytz timeseries strings, as they are not easy to find for the user.

The fix is that the conda-env post-link script has been removed.  It has been replaced by a modified Lib/site.py file in each Python installation, plus a library_bin.pth file in each Python installation.  The combination of these lead to Library/bin being added to os.environ["PATH"] for any Python process.  Any programs you install that end up in Library/bin (for example, patch) will still not be on PATH for older installations.  You can either modify PATH to add Library/bin yourself, or uninstall and reinstall using a new installer (Anaconda >= 2.4.0; Miniconda >= 3.18)

It's not the significant figure accuracy. It's how the HdrHistogram getValueAtPercentile chose to draw the line in the counts. The [current] relevant math is driven by this [java] logic.

final double requestedPercentile = Math.min(percentile, 100.0); // Truncate down to 100%
long countAtPercentile = (long)(((requestedPercentile / 100.0) * getTotalCount()) + 0.5); // round to nearest
countAtPercentile = Math.max(countAtPercentile, 1);

The choice to round the count to the nearest count (as opposed to consistently truncate it or round it up) is the reasons that e.g. the 25%'ile of [1,2,2,2,2] is 1, and the 30%'ile of the same is 2.

There is [probably] a good argument that this is ambiguous and potentially inconsistent with the method's specification. However, since this is how HdrHistogram behaves across various language implementations, I don't want to jump and change this logic quickly. Lets discuss more widely on gitter.

#25 isn't really about using context managers, even though some of the discussion uses that syntax. It's about whether the `commit()` call should appear in the application code or not. 

The reason it's not a context manager is that context managers cannot perform retries: they execute their bodies zero or one times. There's nothing in python that combines reusable code objects with exception handling. You need either a `def` or loop to have code that can be repeated, and then something else to handle exceptions. A non-function-based interface would have to look something like:

```
for attempt in run_transaction(sessionmaker):
    with attempt as session:
        return session.query(M).all()
```

There are some things about this syntax that I like (everything's "in order" and I don't need to name a function), and some things I don't (this is a very unusual pattern and there's no "function signature" to guide you into it, and there's an extra horizontal indent).

So the way I found out about this problem is that our e2e tests exec out in golang to create a deployment. If the timeout for that deployment is exceeded (we dont wnat to wait the full azure timeout) golang attempts to send a `SIG_KILL` to the pid it started. It knew nothing of the spawned processes that the az cli created. Because the cli didnt honor the signal golang will not respond appropriately. 

I would think the best way to handle this is to capture the signals being sent and handle them. In this case if I send a `-9` to the root process you should be able to kill all spawned processes for me.

Lots of thoughts...

The most important cell is the one that has focus. Traditionally we have
called this cell the "selected" cell. It has two purposes or roles:

A) It maintains focus and is the cell that will be edited if the user
presses "Enter".
B) By default cell level actions apply to it.

Previously, we used a single visual indicator for both of these roles (grey
border).

In the new multiple-selection behavior we have agreed upon, these two roles
are being separated.

* The focused cell has both of these roles.
* Contiguous blocks of cells (that include the focused cell) can also have
the second role.

Important things should be indicated as such visually. Less important
things should be indicated as such visually.

In my mind, even with the new behaviors, the focused cell is the most
important one. Actions always apply to to, it is the cell that will be
edited, the page will scroll as the user moves it and it is the cursor/head
for multiple selections. Because of this, it should have the most prominent
visual design.

The other cells to which actions will apply should have a less prominent
visual design than the focused cell as they do fewer things.

My problem with the current design is that the visual design of the action
cells is the equivalent of yelling. The extremely strong visual treatment
of these cells is inconsistent with the more subtle design choices we make
everywhere else *and* it is confusingly more prominent than that focused
cell.

It sounds like you're trying an import in two different installations of Python, or two different environments. Check `sys.executable` to see which Python and environment you're running in, and `sys.path` to see where it looks to import modules.

Python packages must be installed separately for each copy of Python you use, and if you are using virtualenvs or conda envs, packages must be installed into each environment where you need them. Either the package is not installed in one, or a different version of the package is installed.

To install packages to a particular Python installation or environment using pip, you can run it like this:

```shell
path/to/python -m pip install ...
# Replace path/to/python with the path of the Python executable (sys.executable)
```

If you are using conda environments, you can install packages from conda like this:


I'm not entirely sure I grok this proposal. General thoughts below:

> and the way he's setting up ProTect is that generic job functions for tools should be imported and that the pipeline script is mostly just a wrapper wiring these tools together.

I strongly agree with this! I've strived to be like this in the `adam_gatk_pipeline`. Is this similar to what @arkal is planning to do?

However, an important caveat:

> This avoids having to import from another pipeline and centralizes our tool base of job functions.

Oftentimes, you want to import an encapsulated series of jobs from another pipeline. E.g., the `gatk_preprocess` stages are shared between `adam_gatk_pipeline` and `exome_variant_pipeline`.

This may be a terminology nit. What do you (@jvivian) and @arkal define "a pipeline" to be?

My operating definition is that a pipeline = a workflow = a stitched together DAG of jobs. Given that definition, I don't think of STAR or BWA or etc as jobs because they're composed of multiple different jobs (e.g., `download_sample`, `download_indices`, `align_reads`, etc).

> I.E. aligners.py contains job functions like STAR and BWA, quantifiers.py contains job functions like RSEM and Kallisto etc.

I think the answer to my next question hinges on top of the prior terminology question (RE: what is a "job" vs "a pipeline"?), but I'm going anyways. Do you plan to have the aligners in `aligners.py` implement a common interface? This would be something higher level than `Job` —> e.g., `AlignmentJob`.

If the answer is yes, what do these interfaces look like? This problem is fairly straightforward for alignment—all aligners more or less look the same: an aligner takes unaligned reads and an index and produces aligned reads—but looking at quantification, RSEM and Kallisto are drastically different (alignment-based vs. alignment-free, totally different parameters). How do you handle all the tools having different sets of parameters?

If the answer is no, then why are you lumping different tools together into a single module? If it is just for organizational purposes, I would rather not have a single module per tool type. A single package per tool type does seem reasonable though. E.g., `toil.tools.aligners` is a package that contains the `toil.tools.aligners.bwa`, `toil.tools.aligners.star`, etc. modules.

> Common lib: toil_scripts/lib/quantifiers.py, toil_scripts/lib/aligners.py, etc

I don't think that the common lib is a logical home for these tools. When I think "common/core lib", I tend to think of things that are algorithm/problem agnostic.

The way I would think of this for toil is that while we want to use toil for genomics, at the end of the day, nothing about toil is genomics specific. We can use toil for astrophysics pipelines just as well as we can use toil for NLP pipelines just as well as we can use toil for genomics and so on. Thus, functionality that is genomics specific should not go into the core lib —> https://github.com/BD2KGenomics/toil/issues/922.

I do think `toil_scripts` is genomics specific, but I would prefer a different package layout. I would suggest `toil_scripts.tools.x.y` where _x_ is a tool type ("aligner", "quantifier", etc) and _y_ is a specific tool ("RSEM", "Kallisto").

ARMA vs. ARIMA - me being lazy.  I'll change it to `ARIMA` throughout.  SARIMAX is Seasonal Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average eXogenous, which can be done with the same function in statsmodels.  There's reason to think adding seasonal terms (e.g., a 24 hour lag) is appropriate for solar forecasting.  Exogenous inputs are predictors that are not the output being forecast by the ARIMA model, and I don't quite understand how to use them with this function. One challenge is that the SARIMAX function is coded in terms of lags, not timesteps, so to use seasonal terms we'd need to write some additional methods to calculate the lag in terms of the forecast timestep.

My application is short-term, e.g., 1 hour ahead, forecasting of power when the system metadata is not known with confidence.  For what I'm doing, ARIMA is OK.

@alexanderrichards Remember that when a Ganga object is copied, a deep copy is created. Hence, each time a copy of a job is created we create a new sharedir object and thus a new directory. Until any files are added into the sharedir, it is *not* counted by the shareref. The result is that users at the moment have thousand of directories that are not controlled by the sharef counter.

Yes Mark, but then we have to worry about setting and clearing the index cache correctly under different conditions which in my mind introduces more problems.

In the getIndexCache methods I suggest checking if the job is loaded or not in the registry (now possible).
If it is loaded, attempt to generate the cache from memory.
If it's not then first attempt to get the data required from the 'true' index cache which is stored elsewhere, and if that fails revert to loading the job and re-generating the index cache.

Relying on the index cache being empty or the job keeping track of itself leads to design headaches which are better solved by a direct question to the registry to determine if the job is loaded.
The GangaObject shouldn't really have to care about this if the Node is where the IndexCache is stored imo.

I'm extremely against trying to load a job from disk at the start of Ganga. This leads to some severe performance problems with large jobs that I've been trying to mitigate.
Ganga shutdown already does this.

So for now we're going to keep the work private, not because we don't want people to see it, but more so because we are starting completely from scratch and haven't figured out the detailed implementations yet.  There are some issues with the current Cass, e.g. you can't easily make calls to different regions, and the database stuff also leaves something to be desired. We also came up with a totally new way to store/access the data from Riot (which is super cool).

So because the new Cass is so much different, we are starting from scratch and can't just improve the current version of Cass. And because we are still figuring out implementation details, things are changing significantly and quickly (for the last couple weeks, Rob and I have been spending hours and hours talking about things 3-5 nights of the week).

When we get a working framework finished and have some documentation written, we'll likely make it public, at which point people will want to help and we'll have some info to point them at to explain all the complicated stuff that's going on. But until we have something that's worth showing people, we're hesitant to make it public. I hope that makes sense?

I would not completely disable retransmission mechanism even if following set of conditions is met:
- slow platform,
- state with known delay,
- retransmission is caused by retransmitted message from the peer.
I think it would be spec violation. RFC requires to retransmit last flight if previous flight from the peer is received.

I agree we don't have to retransmit a flight multiple times in case there is a few received flights in the buffer. Moreover, mbedtls should process Application Data that is embedded in the handshake message.

BTW There are a few PRs in the mbedtls repository referenced from #1207: https://github.com/ARMmbed/mbedtls/pull/942 https://github.com/ARMmbed/mbedtls/pull/946 . Perhaps it addresses our problems.

In my opinion, it is important to ensure that a code the printer won't be able to render throws an exception. Concerning the checksum and more thorough checks, letting them to the responsibility of the user (many barcode types have only optional checks, some standards like EAN-13 have many variations...) is fine by me. Indeed, it would complexify a lot the code and can be restrictive to some users that just need barcodes for internal purposes. 

What bothers me with the current solution though is the potential difference between the actual barcode and its associated text. 

For example, calling the `barcode` function with the EAN-13 barcode `13243546576` will print the barcode `132435465762` (the ending digit `2` is the checksum computed automatically by the printer), while the printed text is still `13243546576`. 

Another example is the CODE39 standard:
```python
printer.barcode('CODE39', '*WIKIPEDIA*')
printer.barcode('CODE39', 'WIKIPEDIA')
```
Both calls print the same barcode (the start and end characters '*' are automatically added), but the text is different...

What I would propose:

* add a link to the barcode library @MicroJoe mentioned in the documentation
* automatically add checksum to the text _if it is missing_ for EAN and UPC (EAN-13 with only 12 digits -> add checksum)
* automatically add the '*' start/stop characters for CODE39 if missing
* (? make the `compute_checksum` public ?)

I am not sure about other standards, I would need to check.

The predict function return an array of each class that you define with your training set, associated with the probabilities to be true. For an example, We are trying to predict the class of an image, predict will return this array [ 0.9  0.2  0.45  0.56  ] . It means there is 90% that the image is class 1,  20% that it is class 2 .... 56% that it is a class 4 images.
The trick that I found, is to return the index of the maximum of the array returned by predict. The index of the maximum run of the Yvalid set is related to the function to_categorical (references here => http://tflearn.org/data_utils/ )

Currently visdom doesn't support programmatically sizing windows, however we wouldn't be against supporting such functionality moving forward. If you're interested in contributing to such a feature, it would entail adding an additional pair of options (`win_width` and `win_height`) seem appropriate. One tricky thing you may run into looking at the code is that on the frontend windows are referred to as "Panes". Beyond that there are two primary places involved in such a change, the place where windows are packaged into panes, and the place where panes are processed on the frontend:
Windows are packaged into panes [here](https://github.com/facebookresearch/visdom/blob/master/py/server.py#L225). Panes are processed [here](https://github.com/facebookresearch/visdom/blob/master/js/main.js#L105).

> Another thing, transactions has a method called retrieve, while outputs has a method called get, would it make sense in your opinion to unify and use only one term?

The words `retrieve` and `get` are used for `GET` requests such that `retrieve` is for a `GET` endpoint that returns one element whereas `get` is for `GET` requests that return a list of elements.

Basically, the idea is roughly like so:

| HTTP method | driver method |
| ------------------- | -------------------------------- |
| `GET /tx`            | `tx.get()`                        |
| `POST /tx`         | `tx.send()`                      |
| `GET /tx/id`        | `tx.retrieve(id)`                |
| `PUT /tx/id`         | `tx.update(id)`                 |

> All this involves is that the end-user of the driver passes and receives dictionaries, instead of a Transaction model instances.

Not sure how suitable this is. Creating a transfer is not as easy as giving a `transfer` method a couple of input transactions.

See here:
- https://github.com/bigchaindb/bigchaindb-common/blob/master/bigchaindb_common/transaction.py#L407
- https://github.com/bigchaindb/bigchaindb-common/blob/master/bigchaindb_common/transaction.py#L360
- https://github.com/bigchaindb/bigchaindb-common/blob/master/bigchaindb_common/transaction.py#L147

> A lighter weight option would be to promote a number of community members to moderators and try to reach a certain quorum based on how intrusive/useful a new feature is deemed to be before merging it (but then any moderator could do that).

I would definitely prefer a more informal approach, at least at the current level of development, to a more formalized process.  I don't think small fixes and PRs (e.g. the doc fix in #672, or the doc change in baec23c2d4cf542fafc4d26e6d1d8fd3255b3678) need a quorum (or even a PR, for the latter), while larger ones (e.g. #610, #679) benefit from sitting around for a while to attract comments/feedback.

I think as long as there is a general understanding that major features need more review and agreement, and that really shouldn't be merged without having a thorough review by someone else, or while there are outstanding concerns (not just by moderators).  Common sense stuff, really.

My opinion is that I don't have any objection to a formalization of the process, but I also don't think that it's needed with a fairly small number of moderators as long as the process continues working without issues/conflict.

I realized that I gave you false information previously, here:

> Apache plugin seems to be used only as an installer, and standalone plugin is used as an authenticator. In this scenario, Certbot should not be shutting down, restarting or starting up your Apache server, but instead expects the port 443 when using TLS-SNI and port 80 when using HTTP validation to be free.

When Apache plugin is used as installer, it definitely restarts Apache in order to make the renewed certificate to get loaded by Apache. If this wasn't done, Apache would keep serving the old certificate from memory regardless of it being expired.

> So, to change all certificates to use only one type of challenge, is setting the --preferred-challenges to, for example, http sufficient for that, or do we have to do more to achieve a (permanent) change over?

In addition to the challenges, you should standardize the Certbot authentication plugin that's used for renewals, I feel that this is the main issue you are facing. In order to change the used authenticator plugin per certificate lineage you could do the following, I'm assuming you would like to use Apache plugin all of the certificates, as I feel this would be the most robust solution.

You can make the change by issuing the following commands. The renewal configuration file will get updated to reflect these new values by Certbot when run.

First, to figure out which certificate lineages need updating in order to make them all use the same authenticator:
```
$ grep -n authenticator /etc/letsencrypt/renewal/* |grep -v "= apache"
/etc/letsencrypt/renewal/domain.example.org.conf:11:authenticator = standalone
/etc/letsencrypt/renewal/another.example.org.conf:11:authenticator = webroot
```

Now you know that certificate lineages called `domain.example.org` and `another.example.org` are using a non-apache authenticator. The lineage names are inferred from the renewal configuration filename.

You can make them use apache authenticator in future by:

```
certbot renew --cert-name domain.example.org -a apache --preferred-challenges http --force-renewal
```

And the same thing for another one

```
certbot renew --cert-name domain.example.org -a apache --preferred-challenges http --force-renewal
```

Now your certificate lineages should be configured to use HTTP challenges with Apache plugin!

It's a hard question. There certainly are benefits to the both approaches, however I would suggest leaving it as is - to script plugin. This is not up to me, but here are my thoughts about the subject to help you to pick the way to proceed.

Things that would promote merging the functionality with hooks:
 - Code simplicity
 - Being able to possibly use auth and cleanup hooks from inside other plugins also if needed

However things that I see beneficial for having the functionality inside a plugin, be it merged with manual plugin, or left to script plugin:
 - Keeping the authenticator / installer functionality inside plugin, and staying consistent with current design. I think this is also intuitive from the user perspective, as the current hooks are pretty much "one shot" scripts, that are run outside of the actual auth loop, ie: outside of the "magic".
 - In contrary to hooks, plugins are for the actual "heavy lifting" process, while hooks are more like helpers in my view. This is effectively what the authenticator part is doing.

Things promoting keeping it in script plugin instead of merging with manual:
 - The current manual plugin users have either scripted around it or have some kind of process around it. I don't think there are actual conversion factors.
 - Omitting the current manual plugin pause-to-wait-for-challenge-deployment functionality would break scripts for users that have scripted around the manual plugin altogether.

Thanks for the detailed description. Apparently you have a Mifare 1K Tag which is not supported by nfcpy. Mifare 1K belongs to the Mifare Classic family of tags and requires a reader that supports the proprietary Mifare Crypto. While naturally this is built into NXP reader IC's it's not commonly supported by other chipsets. Mifare Classic is also not an NFC Forum Tag Type and nfcpy's main goal is to implement NFC Forum functionality. You would need to look for Mifare Ultralight (NFC Forum Type 2) or Smart MX (Type 4) Tags from NXP's offers. Other possible types are Topaz (Type 1)  from Broadcom and FeliCa (Type 3) from Sony.

I guess this summarize the two approaches.

| | remote ikernel | sos tasks|
| --- | ---- | -----|
| computing engine | always on (living kernel) | use only when needed |
| support for task queue| no | yes |
| multiple jobs | no | yes |
| different kernels | yes, remote kernel can be any kernel or sos | no (sos wrapper of scripts in different languages)|
|multiple hosts | need to start kernel on each host | unlimited |
| local interaction | kernel blocked for large task? | tasks do not block jupyter |
| results | interactive evaluation, results as variables | results can only be returned by files |
|file synchronization| files should be on remote host | automatic synchronization of files |

It depends a bit on how you would like the subtasks to be executed. If you simply want to run them sequentially, we can create a master script to call the task scripts sequentially. @yuyiming Not sure I see the problem here. First, in this case, we will do cleanup only after Batch 2 finishes as well. Before then we have all the GCS lineage so we can reconstruct all the objects and actor states, if needed.

Second, even if Batch 1 finishes and we clean up its state right away things should still work in this case. Say, Batch 1 finishes, its state is cleaned up (i.e., actor states `S1.2` and `S2.1` are checkpointed), and right after this we have a failure that kills Actor 2. In this case Batch 2 can reconstruct the state of Actor 2,  `S2.2.`, by starting from checkpoint `S2.1` and then replaying `T2.1`.

Also, yesterday we did discuss a simple solution that only cleans up the object store. In a nutshell:
- Once a batch finishes we clean up all the objects it has created in the object store, except for the objects passed in `ray.add_return()`.
- We do NOT clean up the GCS. Instead, we let GCS flushing (which is now part of xray) take care of it.

```
in helpdesk/management/commands/get_email.py,  process_queue() :

            msgNum = msg.split(" ")[0]
            logger.info("Processing message %s" % msgNum)
            # added this check :
            raw_content = server.retr(msgNum)[1]
            if type(raw_content[0]) == bytes:
                full_message =  "\n".join([elm.decode('utf-8') for elm in raw_content])
            else :
                full_message = encoding.force_text("\n".join(raw_content), errors='replace')
            #
            ticket = ticket_from_message(message=full_message, queue=q, logger=logger)
```

There is a relatively new tool out there called "Pipenv". This tool is the officially recommended packaging tool according to the Python packaging authority (the guys who maintain PyPI) and has a few benefits over simply declaring the development libraries in a "dev_install.txt" file.

First, Pipenv allows us to declare dependencies for both production and development use, using the new modifiers introduced in PEP 508. Pipenv also allows us to "lock" those dependencies to a particular version and which means we can make lists of dependencies that are known to work. Once we have that list it's extremely easy to deterministically reproduce development or production environments that are guaranteed to work.

Pipenv also layers on top of Python virtual environments. So, when you install a list of dependencies using Pipenv, the tool also automatically creates a virtual environment from a recommended Python version. Activating the virtual environment is as simple as running `pipenv shell` in the project directory.

It is important to note the difference between a strict "packaging" tool and a "package manager". Pipenv is a packaging tool where pip is  package manager. Pipenv does not have the ability to install the bot project from PyPI. Pipenv is instead used to recreate a development environment for users working on developing core bot code. For example, to use pipenv, you would first clone the bot project from GitHub, `cd` into the project directory, and run `pipenv install --dev`. Pipenv would then set up a clean virtual environment for you that has all of the dependencies necessary to perform bot development.

If we do do unlink on close, then it's important to note that `getsockname` is *not* reliable on AF_UNIX sockets. In particular, if you bind a socket and then rename the inode, `getsockname` will return the name you originally bound to, not the name the socket has now.

So one option would be `SocketListener(..., unlink_on_close=None)` / `SocketListener(..., unlink_on_close="/path/to/unlink")`. Another option would be to add a `sockaddr=...` option, which I guess we'll need anyway for #280, and then `unlink_on_close=True` would trust this instead of calling `getsockname`.

Lark cannot read PlyPlus grammar files, but converting them should be relatively straight-forward.

The `%unless` keywords isn't necessary in Lark, it detects these collisions automatically

So:
```python
T_DEFINED_OP : /.[a-zA-Z]+./
T_EQ : ".EQ."
T_NE : ".NE."
```
etc.

Likewise, `%newline` is also unnecessary. Lark detects it automatically.
